name: SEDENA — Mexican Army Combat Forces
detail: Peak deployment of approximately 45,000–50,000 Army soldiers in anti-cartel operations by 2010, up from 6,500 at the drug war's launch in December 2006. Army units operated in all 31 states, assuming public security functions normally handled by police. Army checkpoints, raids, and direct combat became the primary counter-narcotics tool.

name: SEMAR — Navy Special Forces (FES/GOPES)
detail: Mexico's Navy special operations forces conducted the most high-profile takedowns of the Calderón era, including the December 2009 killing of Arturo Beltrán Leyva in Cuernavaca and the October 2012 killing of Zetas founder Heriberto Lazcano. Marines were considered more reliable than Army forces due to tighter internal vetting and were preferred for sensitive intelligence-led operations.

name: Federal Police (Policía Federal)
detail: Founded in 2009 under SSP Secretary Genaro García Luna (later convicted of Sinaloa Cartel bribery), the Federal Police grew to 35,000 officers organized into specialized divisions: organized crime, cybercrime, anti-kidnapping, and border security. Despite later revelations about García Luna's corruption, the Federal Police conducted numerous operations against rival cartels. Disbanded in 2019 under AMLO.

name: UH-60 Black Hawk Helicopters (Mérida Initiative)
detail: The United States transferred UH-60 Black Hawk helicopters to Mexico under the Mérida Initiative for use in rapid-insertion operations against cartel targets. The helicopters were used in the Beltrán Leyva raid in Cuernavaca and numerous other operations. Mexico received 8 Black Hawks in early Mérida deliveries along with other aviation assets including maritime patrol aircraft.

name: CASA CN-235 Maritime Patrol Aircraft
detail: Maritime patrol and surveillance aircraft acquired under the Mérida Initiative enhanced Mexico's ability to track drug shipments from South America across the Pacific and Caribbean. These platforms fed intelligence to combined DEA-SEMAR operations targeting go-fast boats and submarines used for cocaine shipments.

name: DEA — Drug Enforcement Administration (US)
detail: The DEA dramatically expanded its Mexico presence under Calderón, with agents embedded in Mexican operations and sharing real-time intelligence on cartel leadership locations. DEA intelligence was directly credited with enabling the Arturo Beltrán Leyva operation and other high-profile captures. Approximately 70 DEA agents operated in Mexico during peak cooperation, creating friction with Mexican sovereignty concerns.

name: CISEN — National Intelligence Center
detail: Mexico's civilian intelligence agency coordinated with the DEA, CIA, and NSA under the Mérida Initiative framework. CISEN produced threat assessments on cartel leadership and territorial control that informed military deployment decisions. Leaks of CISEN intelligence to cartels — particularly Sinaloa-linked sources within the SSP — compromised several operations.

name: Ion Scanners & X-Ray Inspection Systems
detail: Mérida Initiative funding provided Mexico with advanced ion detection scanners and non-intrusive inspection (NII) X-ray systems for vehicle and cargo screening at border crossings and ports. These systems enhanced detection of drug shipments and weapons trafficking. Training programs accompanied equipment transfers to ensure proper deployment.

name: Federal Police Canine Units
detail: The Federal Police's canine detection units, expanded under the Mérida Initiative with US assistance and animal procurement, were deployed at major highway checkpoints and border crossings to detect narcotics and weapons. Canine units participated in major seizure operations including the historic 23-tonne cocaine haul in 2007.

name: GAFES — Army Special Forces Group (Airborne)
detail: Mexico's elite airborne special forces group conducted high-value target raids throughout the drug war. GAFES units operated in Chihuahua, Tamaulipas, and Sinaloa for kinetic operations against cartel command structures. Notably, many early Zetas leaders were former GAFES who deserted and applied their training to cartel enforcement.
